citation: Lee, J. H., Björling, E., McLean, R., Moreno, A., & Lee, K. J. (2024). Supporting youth mental health through co-designing a virtual reality experience: A recipe-based co-design model. The Library Quarterly: Information, Community, Policy, 94(2), 134-158. https://doi.org/10.1086/729230
summary:
Lee, Björling, McLean, Moreno, and Lee examine a public-library project that combined teen mental-health support, participatory co-design, and virtual reality (VR). The article begins from the COVID-19-era intensification of teen anxiety, depression, isolation, and service gaps, arguing that public libraries may serve as accessible community hubs for wellness-oriented support. The project, launched by the Seattle Public Library and the University of Washington with IMLS support, engaged teens ages 14-18 in designing VR environments intended to support mental health and well-being. Rather than evaluating the finished VR environments, this article focuses on the roles, perceptions, opportunities, and tensions experienced by librarians and library staff who helped facilitate the program.

The study was implemented at three public-library sites: Seattle Public Library, DC Public Library, and Fayette Public Library, Museum and Archives in La Grange, Texas. At each site, librarians or library staff partnered with 10 to 12 teens in co-design sessions that involved reflecting on mental health, imagining supportive VR spaces, and providing feedback on iterative VR prototypes created by undergraduate developer interns. The authors interviewed eight librarians and library staff before and after the program, for 16 interviews total, and also analyzed a community-of-practice discussion using a shared MIRO board. Their modified collaborative thematic analysis focused on staff experience, anticipated and actual challenges, and implications for libraries that want to implement similar programs.

One major finding is that mental health initially felt intimidating to library workers. Many participants wanted to support teen patrons but worried that they lacked training, might say the wrong thing, or might harm participants by mishandling sensitive topics. Over the course of the project, however, staff often shifted from thinking about mental health only in crisis terms to thinking about everyday wellness, stigma reduction, belonging, and safe conversation. The program helped librarians see that library programming can normalize mental-health discussion without making librarians into clinicians. This distinction is important for GBLS: game-based and immersive programs may support care, reflection, and connection while still requiring clear boundaries around professional expertise and referral.

The second major finding concerns co-design. Librarians generally valued youth ownership, collaboration, creative thinking, and the opportunity for teens to see their ideas reflected in a shared product. Some participants even suggested that designing together was itself beneficial for mental health because it required openness, shared goals, and interpersonal connection. At the same time, staff who lacked prior design experience wanted enough structure to facilitate confidently while still needing flexibility to adapt activities, tools, and expectations to local conditions. The authors therefore propose a "recipe-based co-design model" that distinguishes core ingredients that should be preserved from substitutes and local ingredients that can vary. In the model, the main ingredients include the core spirit of co-design, youth participants, and design sprints; substitutes can include different design activities; local ingredients can include community partners or local staff strengths; and tools can include required or optional technologies.

The third major finding is that VR was useful but uneven. VR drew some teens in, supported technology literacy, and offered an immersive way to imagine relaxation spaces, learning spaces, and social spaces. Some staff described social VR as more natural and engaging than ordinary online interaction during pandemic conditions. Yet VR also created barriers: motion sickness, hardware logistics, online technical support, Facebook/Oculus account requirements, variable teen interest, privacy concerns, and the possibility that some marginalized or non-"techie" youth would feel excluded. The authors emphasize that enthusiasm for VR was sometimes overshadowed by enthusiasm for co-design and wellness-oriented programming. This is a crucial caution for GBLS: technology should serve the service goal, not become the goal.

For the GBLS project, the article is useful because it treats immersive technology programming as a staff, service, and community-design problem. It adds evidence to the review's emerging wellness and care section, showing that game-adjacent technologies and design processes can support teen mental-health conversations when framed around everyday well-being rather than clinical intervention. It also belongs in the VR and immersive technology section because it shows both the appeal and the limits of VR in public libraries. Finally, the recipe-based co-design model is broadly useful for game-based library services: it gives libraries a way to preserve core program values while adapting formats, tools, and partnerships to local needs.

The study's limitations are clear. It reports librarian and staff perspectives, not teen outcome measures, long-term mental-health effects, or a formal evaluation of the VR environments. The program was also resource-intensive, involving university partners, developer interns, multiple sites, and substantial technical support. Even so, the article gives GBLS a strong framework for thinking about participatory design, teen services, wellness, staff confidence, and technology adoption. It argues, in effect, that the most transferable element may not be VR itself but the combination of youth voice, flexible facilitation, local adaptation, and reflective program design.

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience: A Recipe-Based Co-design Model
Jin Ha Lee, Elin Björling, Ryan McLean, Anna Moreno, and Kung Jin Lee
ABSTRACT There is a need for librarians to ﬁnd ways to provide service to the youth population regarding mental health. Building on prior research about co-design and virtual reality in public libraries, the research team aimed to create a positive teen mental health experience by co-designing a virtual reality space through the library system. Eight librarians/library staff representing three participating sites were interviewed at the beginning and the end of the project. We discuss various opportunities and difﬁculties that emerged in this project to better understand the role and perspective of librarians and then examine implications for those who are considering implementing similar youth programs at their institutions. On the basis of the ﬁndings, we propose a recipe-based co-design model to better support librarians by allowing ﬂexibility while clarifying which core elements need to be preserved in the co-design process.
T he COVID-19 pandemic brought signiﬁcant changes to people’s lives, including the youth population, who had to adjust to new social distancing restrictions and online/ hybrid schooling. They lost opportunities to interact with their friends in person and had to swiftly adapt to new modes of learning, all while dealing with uncertainties and fear, which had a profound impact on their mental health. These effects were especially impactful to those who were already vulnerable (Courtney et al. 2020; Power et al. 2020). The COVID-19 pandemic has severely exacerbated the negative effects of the lack of mental health support among teens with a steep increase in anxiety and depression among adolescents (Racine et al. 2021). The pandemic also impacted the kinds of library services safely offerable to the general public, including youth. Many libraries had to limit their services, switch to programs that could be offered in an online mode, or close altogether (Cleave and Geijsman 2020; Goddard 2020; Kretz, Payne, and Reijerkerk 2021). There is a pressing need for libraries to ﬁnd ways to continue providing service to the youth population in this challenging time. In

134

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 135
particular, there seems to be an opportunity for public libraries to serve as accessible hubs to support the mental health of youth patrons, as they are well-embedded in various communities and are considered trusted institutions for ﬁnding reliable information (Geiger 2017).
The Seattle Public Library, in collaboration with the University of Washington, launched an initiative supported by the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS) to integrate co-design and virtual reality (VR) technology with the goal of engaging teens, aged 14 to 18, in the design and development of a VR experience focused on supporting and improving their mental health. The number of youth library programs involving co-design has been increasing, as these programs have shown positive impacts on youth by making them feel valued and listened to and offering the opportunity to develop teamwork and leadership skills and contribute to their community (Druin et al. 2009; Yip and Lee 2018). These co-design programs focused on a wide range of digital media, including 3D printing, Arduino, e-textiles, and locationbased games. VR has also been increasingly adopted in libraries for informal learning and social engagement (Lee et al. 2020). VR, in particular, has been shown as an engaging technology for adolescents with the potential to support mental health by reducing stress, promoting mindfulness, and regulating emotions (Flores et al. 2018; Björling et al. 2019). RESeT, a virtual reality environment designed with teens through the Seattle Public Library to help with stress (Björling 2019), and VR programs in Washington State Libraries (Dahya et al. 2021; Lee et al. 2020) are two examples of co-design programs at public libraries incorporating VR. Building on prior research on co-design and VR at public libraries, the research team aimed to create an opportunity for improving youth’s mental health during the prolonged global pandemic.
The co-design program was implemented in three different public libraries. Two of the three were urban public libraries in Seattle and Washington, DC, with the third being a rural public library in La Grange, Texas. This paper examines our understanding of the perspective and role of librarians and library staff involved in this project, while the detailed analysis of the design process and outcome will be shared elsewhere. We interviewed librarian and library staff participants representing all three sites before and after the VR program.
RQ1: What do librarians and library staff perceive as the opportunities and challenges of offering youth co-design programs to support mental health?
RQ2: What tensions emerged during the co-design program, and what are the implications for librarians and library staff considering implementing such programs?
Relevant Literature Mental Health, Teens, and Libraries
We are facing a teen mental health crisis in the United States. Nearly 10% of adolescents in the United States, estimated at 2.3 million, suffer from depression, with minority and poor youth

136 • The Library Quarterly
disproportionately affected (Giota and Gustafsson 2017; National Institute of Mental Health [NIMH] 2019; Ivey-Stephenson et al. 2020). Exacerbating this crisis is the fact that mental health care demands far exceed capacity, leaving a large proportion of adolescents without access to mental health services (NIMH 2019; Golberstein et al. 2020; Ivey-Stephenson et al. 2020). The need for community-based support for adolescent mental health has never been more dire, as the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic have exacerbated an already dire mental health situation. Globally, clinical rates of anxiety and depression have doubled in adolescents during the COVID-19 pandemic (Racine et al. 2021), which prompted the declaration of a national emergency (American Academy of Pediatrics 2021). As a consequence of pandemicrelated school closures, social isolation, and loneliness, adolescent mental health issues, including rates of depression (Wang et al. 2021), have surged.
As an accessible, community-based setting, libraries are uniquely positioned as potential hubs to access mental health support. Mental health is already emerging as a topic of interest in many communities: In a recent survey of 50 different library institutions in the United Kingdom, 75% said they had taken on some activity to support mental health and well-being during the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic (Cox and Brewster 2020). Seventy-nine percent of respondents said that their librarians were offered short courses run by their university on mental health and well-being. Given the scale of the mental health concerns in adolescents, it is likely impossible for librarians serving teens to avoid the topic of mental health, yet only 17% of libraries indicated that staff were formally trained on mental health. Sixty-eight percent of libraries said that building a sense of belonging was a main issue they tried to address during the pandemic, and 20% said they were actively working to reduce stigma around mental health. Taken together, the ﬁndings suggest that librarians are interested in providing mental health support for the patrons but lack sufﬁcient training and administrative support to do so conﬁdently.
Participatory Design and Libraries
Participatory design (PD) is a user-centered design method (Sanders 2002) that has been adopted and utilized in libraries to develop various programs and digital learning experiences (Yip, Lee, and Lee 2020). Prior examples include various stakeholders such as students, faculty, and librarians coming together to design physical or virtual learning commons (Somerville and Collins 2008; Santos et al. 2017), digital learning activities (Yip et al. 2020), and organizational structures and communication systems (Somerville and Howard 2010) and develop a public library (Dalsgaard and Eriksson 2013).
Collaboration between youth and librarians often occurs in the form of advisory boards where the youth advise librarians on the design of programs. Linda Camino (2005) points out several challenges with this approach, namely the potential over-dependence on youth to run the programs, adults not yielding power to youth, and bias linked to stereotypes about

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 137
youth. Camino discusses three key elements for successful youth and adult partnership, which are fundamental in participatory librarianship with youth: 1) learning communities (Senge 1991) as a group of people who help each other build new skills and capabilities; 2) communities of inquiry where people redeﬁne their roles to be both creators and users (Friedman 2006); and 3) communities engaging in reﬂection in practice (Schön 1987). Jason C. Yip, Kung Jin Lee, and Jin Ha Lee (2020) also discuss how the librarian’s role shifts between “supporter of youth” and “designer with youth” with regard to four different aspects of design: relationship building, facilitation, design-by-doing, and idea elaboration. They argue that frequent transitions occur between these roles during the co-design process and emphasize the importance of providing pre-service librarians opportunities to participate in a realistic participatory design process.
Additionally, with the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent shift to online programming in many libraries, Kung Jin Lee et al. (2021) present a conceptual model of conducting synchronous participatory design with youth, building upon theories of improvisation. They suggest that key features of improvisation—including reﬂexivity, transgression, tension, listening, and interdependence—play a critical role in online synchronous co-design, which poses additional uncertainties and challenges compared to in-person co-design. Two of the three library sites in our study also had to engage in online synchronous co-design, and thus, a prominent need for improvisation emerged (as further discussed).
Teens, Libraries, and VR
There has been a long interest in understanding teens’ perceptions of libraries. By using both quantitative and qualitative methods, Vivian Howard (2011) shared ﬁndings about teens’ perceptions of libraries. While the survey results showed overall satisfaction, the focus group analysis showed critical dissatisfaction about barriers to relationships between library staff and teens. Studies have called out the importance of responding to the teens’ needs to foster and maintain habits of library use, indicating a need for better technology access (Agosto et al. 2016) and more opportunities to connect with tools that can leverage their learning in the library (Braun et al. 2018). Among the different tools, VR has been a popular modality for teens due to the social engagement opportunities and broad immersive activities within VR (Maloney, Freeman, and Robb 2021). As interest in VR continues to grow through social media, gaming, and entertainment industries, more public libraries are offering an opportunity for their patrons to experience VR through VR-speciﬁc programming (Lee et al. 2020; Dahya et al. 2021). Public libraries serve as a community hub in a wide variety of communities, including rural and urban areas and thus are an excellent venue for disseminating new technologies, such as VR, to the general public. VR is also being adopted in more academic libraries (Pope 2018; Frost et al. 2020) and specialized libraries, such as health sciences libraries (Lessick and Kraft 2017), for its unique learning opportunities and user engagement.

138 • The Library Quarterly
Despite the opportunities that VR provides, many challenges still exist. Some are related to technology; for instance, Negin Dahya et al. (2021) point out that inequalities embedded in the technology itself, as well as perceptions of the technology, resulted in unequal access for marginalized groups such as women or immigrants in their study. Kung Jin Lee et al. (2020) also discuss issues related to the experience of VR in a public place like the library, where not everyone feels safe or comfortable being on public display. Practical challenges, such as how to deal with multiple people sharing the VR headset, scheduling the use of VR, setting limited time for each patron, and providing support for the play session to ensure safety for the patron, were also mentioned as hurdles in a public setting. For the future direction of the VR programs in libraries, Lee et al. (2020) discuss the importance of library patrons participating in the design of VR content beyond accessing VR as consumers; our project attempts to include youth patrons in libraries to co-create VR content without requiring extensive technical knowledge or coding background.
Study Design and Method Motivation
This research study was part of a larger project to explore the co-design of a VR environment intended to support teen mental health. Three library sites (Seattle Public Library, DC Public Library, and Fayette Public Library, Museum and Archives) engaged teens in learning about VR technology and designing their own VR environments through their public library system. We wanted to understand librarians’ experiences being part of this program and how their perspectives changed as a result. To answer this question, we employed qualitative pre- and post-program interviews.
Program
The focus of the program was to create a positive teen mental health experience by codesigning a VR space through the library system. The goal of the librarians was to create close relationships with teens and community stakeholders by centering the discussion on mental health and providing a digital learning experience. The three pillars that guide the projects are: teen mental health, co-design, and VR.
At each of the three library sites, librarians (including library staff) partnered with a group of 10 to 12 teens and engaged in a series of co-design sessions reﬂecting on mental health and generating ideas on how the VR program could support their mental health and well-being. All the teens were between 14 and 18 and had a range of experiences with VR, with some completely new to VR while others had played VR games with friends. They partnered with undergraduate student interns who worked as the VR developers. These interns took ideas generated from the co-design sessions to create successive iterations of the actual VR experience. These iterations were demoed for the teens, who provided feedback for subsequent changes.

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 139
There were two interns assigned to each site from the University of Washington and Howard University.
Librarian Sample
This study included eight librarians (including library staff) across the three sites. The eight librarians were interviewed at the beginning and the end of the project for a total of 16 interviews. All the librarians had experience working with teen patrons, and two also had experience with co-design and VR.
Individual Interviews
We conducted semi-structured interviews with the librarians via Zoom prior to beginning and after the completion of the program. The pre-program interviews focused on the librarians’ expectations, opinions, perceived values, and comfort level with implementing a new VR co-design program for supporting mental health with youth. We also elicited concerns about the program. The post-program interviews invited librarians to reﬂect on their initial concerns and to share any insights that arose during or after the conclusion of the program. The interview protocol is included in the appendix. Questions included: Given your original concerns and impressions about the project, what has surprised you based on your experience? Given your experience, has your level of comfort changed regarding running library programs to support patrons’ mental health? Interviews were recorded and transcribed for data analysis.
Community of Practice Discussion
Another data source for this analysis was a “Community of Practice” discussion with project staff and librarians. In this discussion, the project team was asked to use a shared MIRO board (ﬁg. 1), a platform for online collaboration, to reﬂect on what went well and what they would change about the program. Example questions include: What are some lessons learned through your experience implementing this program? What are the biggest likes you experienced while implementing this program? What are some design ideas you have for making the program more engaging, impactful, and/or easier to implement?
Qualitative Analysis
A modiﬁed, collaborative thematic analysis (Clarke et al. 2015) was conducted in which four of the ﬁve authors collaboratively analyzed the data. The authors conducted all four phases of a reﬂexive, thematic analysis including: familiarization, coding, theme generation, and theme review. During familiarization, authors were guided by the research question “What was the experience of library staff during this program?” and assigned individual interviews and associated transcripts to explore for salient excerpts. Initial codes were then applied to these excerpts to help describe participant experiences. All excerpts and codes were captured in a

140 • The Library Quarterly
Figure 1. Screenshot of MIRO board showing a sample question used in the Community of Practice discussion
shared MIRO board to facilitate a collaborative analysis. These excerpts and associated codes were then collaboratively reviewed, discussed, and organized by all the authors until core themes emerged and were formalized (theme generation). Example themes include “apprehension” and “the library as a community.” Upon writing the current paper, these data were organized into more practical, categorical sections/themes in an effort to summarize successes and challenges, utilizing direct excerpts from several of the themes.
Findings Opportunities and Challenges
In this section, we answer the ﬁrst research question on librarians’ perceptions of opportunities and challenges in offering youth co-design programs to support mental health. At the start of the project, many librarians expressed potential challenges related to this project, including facilitating programs with a mental health aspect, conducting co-design with teens, and the

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 141
novelty of VR technology. In addition, the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic occurred after libraries had committed to the program but before the actual program began, adding another layer of challenge and uncertainty. Fortunately, each of these challenging elements provided learning opportunities for project team members, engagement among the teen participants, and an overall sense of value for the librarians involved. Below, we illustrate each challenge area as well as how those challenges were overcome.
Mental Health Support Programs at Libraries
Librarians’ attitudes notably shifted when comparing pre- to post- interviews. When asked about their roles, experiences, and concerns in “supporting patron mental health,” many pre-interviews described prior or anticipated future situations where patrons were suffering from poor mental health. These interviews revealed a high desire to support patrons but low conﬁdence in their ability to effectively help patrons in these circumstances. After learning about mental health during the course of the program, many librarians expressed a shift in perception to seeing mental health issues as something that all people experience and believing that an approach supportive of mental health and well-being could be adopted by many library services.
Overall, the greatest concern librarians expressed was engaging with the topic of mental health. This was true even for librarians with prior experience in co-design, VR, and teen programming. The majority of facilitators voiced a similar concern that “saying something wrong” during the course of co-design exercises might inadvertently harm the teen participants, although both the “saying something wrong” and harm were ambiguous. A commonly cited contributing factor to this concern was a lack of prior training or education speciﬁc to mental health, which may have created a general insecurity about engaging in this topic. Librarians P4 and P2 shared, “[I am] afraid of, sort of like upsetting the people who are participating and doing more harm than good . . . we have a social worker for a reason” (P4, Pre-Interview); “you don’t want to be in that paralyzing position and you want to be able to help youth correctly” (P2, Pre-Interview).
However, Librarian P8 discussed how broadening the librarian’s perspectives on mental health to include wellness could potentially help them feel more comfortable about participating in the program:
I think it really depends on the program itself, I know that I am not like a medical professional so I can’t give speciﬁc advice as to this is how you need to be dealing with this issue that you have, but if it’s about wellness, if it’s about how do you, you know ﬁnd peace, how do you ﬁnd things that relax you, that I’m perfectly comfortable with . . . if it’s something to bring awareness, or to bring familiarity on the topic . . . getting the knowledge necessary to identify any concerns you may have. (P8, Pre-Interview)

142 • The Library Quarterly
At the beginning of the project, the research team gave a presentation about mental health and co-design to support the librarians. In the post-project interviews, multiple librarians shared how this presentation helped broaden their overall perspectives on mental health, helped “destigmatize/normalize mental health,” provided tools to “address mental health without being too ‘heavy,’” and provided useful vocabulary for discussing the topic, according to notes from the MIRO board. Librarian P8 stated, “This idea of mental health being on a sort of—not being binary—is not you either have it or you don’t, but being on this scale of you know, maybe sometimes it’s a little lower, sometimes it’s a little higher, so just that framework really helped guide a lot of the decisions” (P8, Post-Interview).
The concerns around addressing mental health also eased once the program and co-design sessions began. Reﬂecting on this, several librarians explained that while they had originally associated “mental health support” with crisis situations such as teen self-harm and suicide, during the sessions they were able to shift their perspective toward everyday mental health and well-being: “Going through this project, it really made me think, it’s easier to talk about mental health than I thought, because we do it all the time, naturally, and it was kind of cool to get that perspective of thinking about mental health as a spectrum, which makes total sense” (P2, Post-Interview).
Librarian P7 talked about a turning point when they realized that, rather than creating an intervention, the goal of the program was to create a space where youth and librarians could talk about mental health. (“Teens want to talk about and design around mental health,” from the MIRO board.)
Multiple librarians felt that libraries had an important role in their communities and expressed the importance of listening to and understanding patron needs and using this knowledge to tailor the library’s programming, including the types of services and technology access the library prioritizes. The need to address mental health, especially for youth patrons, was seen as inevitable and important, as P6 shares, “[In my] experience of just working with teens, it kind of inevitably crosses over into mental health, just because it’s such an emotionally turbulent time and without a doubt you’re going to have some teen coming in and maybe they’re being bullied or something like that, they’re just going to talk about it at some point” (P6, PreInterview). Facilitating the co-design sessions gave librarians a ﬁrst-hand conﬁrmation of the potential demand, at least among teens, for programs supporting mental health. Further, librarians acknowledged the potential role librarians and libraries could play in democratizing mental health care. Librarians stated,
Yeah, I mean I think it’s super important to be able to, you know, maybe democratize some of these skills and some of these frameworks and information. Because again, like you think about the topic mental health and you automatically assume that someone in the room needs to be an expert to talk about it, or to be able to facilitate a discussion,

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 143
but that’s not the case at all. As soon as we started to just open up spaces, especially in places like libraries, it really helps. (P8, Post-Interview)
Hypothesis: when given the space, teens will create things that are good for their mental health. (MIRO board)
Librarian P1 also had the realization while working with teens that librarians could potentially create more space for mental health for their patrons and provide the time and space to talk about difﬁcult topics.
I don’t think that we fully grasp how they are feeling and how they reﬂect on that. We have not provided them enough time and space to communicate those things, and by not doing that, they simply don’t think about it. [. . .] these [mental health] are things that you have to be proactive about because we’re so used to be trained to be reactive. We’re trained to, okay, if this happens call this person, do this, or refer them to somebody, or God forbid call their parent, [. . .] we need to create that safe space where that’s something that we can talk about all the time. (P1, Post-Interview)
P3 also reﬂected on the relationship between public libraries and social services and how library staff’s work inherently becomes tied to mental health:
The way public libraries actually function, because they are one of the last community spaces left . . . it’s very much part of the fabric of social services . . . library staff end up being [at] one of the places where people with no training on how to deal with people being on the edge or experiencing problems that affect social behavior and even personal behavior . . . we really have to sort of deal with it and navigate that world . . . It’s been on my mind a lot, how much of what we do really is about everyone’s mental health and how that is also tied to a lot of other factors. (P3, Post-Interview)
Co-design of VR Content
Many librarians expressed apprehension about their ability to conduct co-design sessions and/ or learning to use the actual features and functionalities of the devices. Many expressed a desire for highly detailed, structured program support information. Some libraries did encounter challenges with the technology, but these tended to be technology adjacent to the VR devices (laptops, internet connections, etc.) or mundane aspects of the technology (cables, batteries) relative to what was expected. Attitudes toward co-design were more divergent after the program. When a sense of shared creation could be attained, co-design was seen as highly valuable and enabling, both in engaging teens and in supporting mental health. Co-design was perceived

144 • The Library Quarterly
as the key enabler, and more so than VR. Some libraries experienced technical challenges and were inhibited from reaching a sense of shared creation by these challenges.
Most of the librarians in this cohort said that their formal graduate program education did not include design methods coursework. While two mentioned prior experience with codesign, the remaining six could not recall any such signiﬁcant experience. When asked directly if they “saw” or considered themselves to be designers, all responded no, including those who mentioned prior work that could reasonably be considered co-design, such as creating services alongside patrons. One of the librarians (P2, Pre-Interview) described design/co-design as being “very speciﬁc, for a speciﬁc group,” and another struggled to identify with the term “designer.” Librarian P3 explained, “I always see myself as a passive observer of the design process. Sometimes I feel like I’m a little too analytical to get my hands dirty and jump in. I have done it, but [designer] is not a label I attach to myself” (P3, Pre-Interview).
Despite a lack of co-design experience or design identity, the majority of librarians anticipated that engaging in co-design would create positive outcomes for the teen teams. Librarians anticipated teens increasing their ability to collaborate and work in teams through autonomy and ownership of their ideas brought by co-design. Librarian P4 shared, “Co-design helps develop a number of different skill sets that are important for a teen as they enter into adulthood, so you know there’s critical thinking, creative thinking” (P4, Pre-Interview).
The primary co-design challenge was the anticipation of a scenario where the lesson plans lacked enough structure and details to enable the librarians to facilitate co-design effectively. Librarians also expressed a discomfort in the general uncertainty and lack of a predetermined outcome inherent in co-design. Librarians expressed a desire for more structure and planning, which they believed would help with the success of the program. Librarian P5 shared their expectation for co-design: “The structure has to be very carefully presented for it to be effective. [. . .] [It] really has to be thought out and all of the ways in which people connect or don’t connect have to be certain. [. . .] And then there’s some accountability, of course. [. . .] Without accountability, without real engagement from all parties, you’re not really at co-design” (P5, Pre-Interview).
However, during the co-design experience, several adjustments were made to ensure that everyone was able to participate effectively and comfortably. For instance, at the Seattle Public Library site, initially the design activities consisted of VR sessions that ranged from 45 minutes to an hour. Feedback collected after earlier sessions revealed that some participants were experiencing VR-induced motion sickness, especially when using apps with lateral movement. Responding to this feedback, the design team adjusted future sessions to reduce the amount of continuous time participants spent within VR, opting instead to expand the use of digital tools like MIRO as well as “ofﬂine” tools like sketching on paper. Other accommodations included swapping VR apps that the group found difﬁcult to use (e.g., Mozilla Hubs) with alternatives (e.g., CoSpaces). We believe this responsiveness to early suboptimal experiences helped

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 145
sustain engagement from the teens at a level that librarians noted as higher than teen programs they had facilitated previously. P3 additionally shared how having a co-design participant who experienced the program at another site helped with the process:
We had a really hard time until [the student researcher] came and helped us out. While we had watched some of the recordings [but] none of us had participated in something like this before . . . I always feel in co-design you need at least one person to be exactly on the guide-post and where you want to be hitting to make sure you’re always moving in that direction even if how you get there is different. You need to know where you’re supposed to be at the end . . . The technology wasn’t super difﬁcult; we were able to get that down. It was the co-design piece for us [that was difﬁcult]. (P3, Post-Interview)
P4 also noted that once they were given the structure to facilitate the co-design process, they initially felt the lack of ﬂexibility in the process (“The templates made it so much easier but needs a bit more ambiguity to mold to each library’s needs” [MIRO board]) but were able to successfully run the sessions after clarifying the co-design goal and adaptable elements: “[For] each session, okay, this is what we’re hoping to accomplish in this session and we’re like, okay, so . . . as long as we accomplish this, we’re good” (P4, Post-Interview).
In interviews conducted at the conclusion of the project, the majority of librarians described their experiences positively. P3 was content that the co-design practices were getting integrated into their normal programming activity, and they were using it more in the organization. P8 shared that they were surprised by how positive the co-design experience was, despite their initial concern about doing co-design via Zoom:
I was pleasantly surprised that we were able to ﬁnd tools like MIRO that allowed us to collaborate, even if it is in this very different environment, you know. I wasn’t able to see a lot of the teen’s faces as they were somewhat shy at the beginning, and so I was surprised [by] the level of engagement we were able to sustain for as long as we did, through these co-design activities. I think that was really a cornerstone of making every meeting really fun and that they were able to derive value from each one of our meetings. (P8, Post-Interview)
Some of the challenges that emerged in co-design related to the nature of current VR content development and tools. For this cohort, groups were partnered with undergraduate VR developer interns who helped realize the teens’ design ideas in the VR space through an iterative process. This process allowed teens to participate in VR content creation even without extensive coding experience. While this back-and-forth slowed the design process compared to if the teens had been directly creating their ﬁnal deliverables, the librarians felt

146 • The Library Quarterly
that working with VR provided a unique co-design opportunity not just for teens but also for undergraduate interns who worked as professional developers: P8 noted that “it’s completely immersive so you can take a teen out of whatever situation they’re in and for a brief second put them in any sort of environment . . . have them be in a relaxation space or have them be in a learning space or just a fun space so it’s just that embodied experience that is so transformative in your learning” (P8, Pre-Interview). The MIRO board also showed comments regarding biggest likes such as: “seeing the excitement of the teens about their ﬁnal product”; “giving undergraduate students opportunities to have a real world [development] experience.”
One librarian, P1, suggested that co-design participants postulated that the co-design itself, which requires participants to open up, share ideas, and work together toward a goal, was supporting teen mental health: “And we were talking about mental health and co-design and VR and everything like that. One of the teams even said, well, I think, designing together was beneﬁcial for my mental health” (P1, Post-Interview).
Value of VR in Library Programming
Considering VR in the larger scope of library programming, prior to this program, many librarians had been looking forward to exploring how VR and its capabilities might beneﬁt patrons. Several framed the relationship between libraries, communities, and novel technology like VR as symbiotic, expected, and common. Concerns around VR were more systemic in nature, such as difﬁculties common to bringing any new technology into the library, or inequality in access to technology within their communities. Librarians were somewhat undecided on whether they imagined themselves incorporating VR into future programming. While the overall sentiment was positive, the enthusiasm for VR seemed to be overshadowed by enthusiasm toward co-design and incorporating supportive approaches toward mental health into library services and programming.
As used in this program, VR was described as both “engaging” as well as “a potential barrier across settings.” Most librarians agreed that VR development supported the library agenda of technology literacy for both patrons and librarians. However, many also felt that VR technology was overwhelming at times, even with substantial technical support. The following quote from the MIRO board suggests substantial time is needed for preparing to use, rather than learning how to use, VR: “The amount of time it took to plan sessions and prepare the technology for each session (was longer than expected); the amount of time it took for the teens and staff to learn the technology (was shorter than expected)” (MIRO board).
Some librarians found that VR was powerful in drawing teens into the library and co-design program. P4 and P5 shared how much they enjoyed the VR experience with the teens, especially during the pandemic, when physical gatherings were restricted and online interactions in apps like Zoom felt limited:

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 147
We were in VR chat, like that was probably the most fun that we had, like that was the most natural interaction we had, and it was really cool, like you know, playing with them in that space. (P4, Post-Interview)
To use VR was a really interesting way to co-design, speciﬁcally in that, it was like manipulating what feels like a real space, so it’s almost like designing in the community or designing physical space which is something I think a lot of people don’t think about, but they have a really fun time with. (P5, Post-Interview)
Biggest likes: combining everyone’s thoughts into one VR experience. (MIRO board)
However, one librarian suggested that VR was not the reason teens were engaged in the program. P1 suggested that although VR increased tech literacy within their library, it was not the drawing point for participation, and mentioned that they felt it was excluding some teens. While this librarian could recognize that their comfort with VR technology had improved, the added value of it for teen programming was less apparent: “So as far as VR, yes, I feel a lot more comfortable with it, since I had no experience to begin with, but I don’t know that we would use VR again as the method [to draw in teens]” (P1, Post-Interview). The ideas from the MIRO board on how to make the program more engaging (e.g., “More implementation of mental health ideas and mapping them to VR interactions”) suggest that perhaps some librarians experienced difﬁculties understanding how the VR content being provided had connections to mental health. They felt the need to explore more content with the teens and make connections of how the interaction within VR would inﬂuence the teen’s mental health ideas (“more exploration in VR—have teens involved in picking games/experiences to play together”). Further, they pointed out the varying degree of comfort and expertise with VR technology among the teens and suggested “leveled activities for teens and groups that are more comfortable with the tech and VR mechanics.”
Additionally, a community member who worked with ethnically diverse youth suggested to P1 that a VR program was not going to meet the interests of his community of teens. P1 added that there could be other programs the youth would be interested in that could still teach skills: “It’s just that this [VR] was not what was going to draw them in. And it wasn’t what they felt they needed.” This sentiment aligned with what Dahya et al. (2021) discovered in their VR project with Washington State Libraries; they argued for a more active and intentional recruiting approach to include users from marginalized groups, because patrons often tend to view VR as a gaming tool that is targeted for “techie” people and not for them. They suggest connecting VR with larger library events, making it part of a themed activity (e.g., art, science), and designing interactive wraparound programming with other related activities that could help make it more enticing to wider audiences. VR also introduced some privacy challenges. Librarians encountered some parents/caregivers who were hesitant to let the youth

148 • The Library Quarterly
participate because they had to sign up and obtain a Facebook account to be able to use the Oculus Quest VR headset.
Finally, librarians noted the potential complexity of incorporating professional VR developers into the design process. For example, the external developer may have detracted from a sense of ownership in the project design (e.g., misinterpreting some of the teens’ ideas) or caused a delay in the VR development cycle, which may have detracted from the natural ﬂow of collaborative co-design.
Overall, the VR aspect garnered mixed reactions from the librarians. Some felt very enthusiastic about its appeal to teens and expressed desires to continue incorporating it in their future services, while others felt uncertain as to whether they will continue to include it in teen programming.
Implications for Future Programs
In this section, we answer the second research question on tensions that emerged during the co-design programs and the implications for librarians considering implementing similar programs, proposing a recipe-based co-design model.
Role of Librarian and Libraries in Providing Mental Health Support
Establishing libraries as places to provide mental health support presents a huge opportunity to improve the mental health of teens. Teens rarely have easy access to mental health resources, especially teens who live in rural areas or are part of marginalized communities (US Department of Health and Human Services 2001). Even where there is greater access, stigma against seeking mental health support in communities or cultures could still prevent people from seeking help (Bulanda et al. 2014). Because of the way libraries are embedded into many communities, it is common for librarians and teens to regularly see each other, leading to a sustained relationship (Subramaniam 2016), as with a teacher, coach, or another community mentor. Library programming allowing teens to discuss mental health with librarians might help normalize mental health support as a regular, everyday matter. Rather than just addressing crisis situations, mental health support might be considered similar to health activities “for all,” such as daily walks, eating well, and getting quality sleep. Despite initial concerns and hesitancy, the librarians who participated in our program were unanimous in their support of continuing to offer similar programs focused on mental health. Moreover, librarians also speculated that facilitating such programming might help improve their own mental health as well.
Value of Co-design Process
The co-design process provided a context for teens and adults to engage in conversations around mental health openly and comfortably. While the design outcomes were important

Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience • 149
contributors to participants’ sense of accomplishment, the design process itself was also very important. Librarians built rapport and connections through the co-design process and learned that others also experience challenges and employ coping strategies similar to their own. Learning about what fellow participants found comfort in and liked throughout the co-design process allowed participants to incorporate those elements in the design outcomes for others and establish connections. Librarians shared how being vulnerable by sharing their own personal stories and feelings about mental health seemed to encourage teens to do the same. Most librarians also noted how successful the program was in terms of the continued high engagement from teen participants. The interview data suggests that an important factor for that success was the connections participants built by engaging in design activities together.
It is important to note that the sense of design ownership, a key value of the co-design process, may have been unintentionally compromised for teens because of challenges related to VR. Although VR provided an engaging and novel platform to work with for some sites, for other sites, adopting this technology may have excluded some teens. Given the desire for inclusivity and diverse teen engagement within public libraries, it is important to consider the possibility of excluding teens uncomfortable with or uninterested in VR.
A Recipe-Based Co-design Model
One of the biggest challenges librarians expressed relative to co-design was ﬁnding the right amount of structure to provide conﬁdence to librarians with little co-design experience, while leaving enough ﬂexibility and freedom for all librarians to adapt the program to their speciﬁc contexts. The co-design process at the Seattle Public Library site was considered very successful both in terms of youth engagement and the outcome. Based on interviews and observation of the co-design sessions, a major contributing factor may have been that the librarians at this site had prior co-design experience. As difﬁculties arose, these librarians were able to adapt and “improvise” (Lee et al. 2021) as needed without hesitance, changing aspects of co-design on the ﬂy in response to contextual factors such as technology, setting, and participant feedback. In initial interviews and conversations that occurred as part of the program, librarians from other sites expressed a desire for detailed structure and plans, which they felt important to help them get started. Later, some of this structure came to be perceived as restrictive rather than enabling—especially where techniques or tools did not seem to ﬁt local circumstances. For librarians less familiar with co-design, it seemed unclear both “how” and “how much” they needed to either adhere to or adjust the provided plan.
Based on this ﬁnding, we propose the analogy of a “recipe” for future co-design curriculum, which we hope will support further adoption of co-design among libraries with varied levels of co-design experience. In cooking, recipes describe how to reproduce certain dishes and are often written with the assumption that individuals have no prior experience making that dish.

150 • The Library Quarterly
Even lacking in experience, a cook may be able to substitute ingredients called for in the recipe while still producing an outcome true to the original nature of the dish, so long as those substitutions are thoughtfully made. Meanwhile, other seemingly minor substitutions may cause the recipe to fail. The best recipes guide the chef not just in the “model” way of making the dish but also by providing allowable substitutions.
Adapting this model to co-design, a recipe-based curriculum would make clear which elements librarians should carefully follow and retain and which elements can be changed without compromising the goal or outcome of the session. For example, the core spirit of co-design might be described as involving all parties as active participants, with librarians modeling a facilitation role. Some common substitutions might include the speciﬁc tools (physical or digital) used for communication and capturing and reﬁning of ideas, and the nature and form of the ﬁnal deliverable from the co-design experience. Framing co-design in this way also allows the co-design team to ask a more fundamental question: What is most important for the speciﬁc project, the process of cooking together or the dish produced? In other words, is the project more focused on the collaborative process itself, the design outcome, or both? If the process is more important, we can substitute even more to produce a different (but equally ﬁne) dish than originally anticipated. In other cases, producing a particular dish may be important and the cook needs to ensure the main ingredients remain intact. In the context of co-design, for instance, is it okay if the team ends up producing a collaborative art creation activity instead of a VR game or not? Thinking about co-design with the recipe-based model can help the librarians balance project structure and ﬂexibility in a more practical manner. We expect the co-design recipe to specify the following elements:
• Dish: the design goal and outcome • Ingredients: elements needed for preparing the co-design session
◦ Main ingredients: things that cannot be substituted, such as the core spirit of codesign or speciﬁc participants
◦ Substitutes: things that can be replaced, such as alternative co-design approaches, strategies, and activities. Ideally, a number of alternatives should be provided.
◦ Local ingredients: strengths of the speciﬁc libraries or librarian leading the co-design, such as expertise/knowledge in speciﬁc domain, strong rapport with youth participants, etc.
• Cooking tools: tools and technologies needed for the co-design session ◦ Required tools: critical tools/technologies that must be included ◦ Optional tools: tools and technologies that can be replaced. Ideally, it should provide a number of optional tools/technologies.
An example of a recipe-based co-design may look like table 1.

Conclusion
Through VR co-design sessions involving teens at multiple public libraries, we were able to identify opportunities and challenges related to designing and implementing such programs, especially related to supporting teen mental health. While mental health was initially perceived by librarians as an intimidating topic to engage in with teens, the librarians’ perceptions shifted throughout the program. Librarians reﬂected on the roles of libraries and librarians in supporting teen mental health and ultimately showed greater conﬁdence and interest in offering library programming related to mental health and well-being. VR improved the appeal of the program to some teens, and the social aspect of VR was seen as a major draw. Some challenges that arose included identifying appropriate collaboration tools, providing technical support exclusively through online/virtual interaction, and the potential for excluding certain participants. This resulted in mixed responses regarding future VR-related library programming. Unlike VR, co-design was unanimously seen as a valuable approach to allowing teens to actively participate in a project and see their input in the design process reﬂected in the outcome. However, librarians who lacked prior co-design experience expressed seemingly conﬂicting desires; they wanted detailed structure to get the project started but were impeded by the level of structure as they encountered unexpected situations.
On the basis of the ﬁndings from this study, we propose a recipe-based co-design model as an approach to better support co-design, especially for librarians who are interested in adopting

152 • The Library Quarterly
co-design in their programs but are less familiar with co-design methods. This approach enables librarians to see what core elements need to be preserved in the co-design process and highlights areas where creative adaptation can be deployed as needed to overcome technical and logistical challenges. This may be especially valuable for projects with multiple sites to help ensure consistent quality of programming across the sites, regardless of local contexts and resources.
In our future work, we plan to continue building upon this project and work with other library sites to offer additional co-design programs focused on teens’ mental health and wellbeing. We will be adopting and testing the recipe-based co-design model to investigate whether and how our model can help librarians feel more conﬁdent about running a co-design program and do it productively, even if the librarians are new to co-design.
